title: Crafting a Minimal React Portfolio That Still Feels Alive
excerpt: How I kept the bundle small while layering in scroll-triggered reveals, hover choreography, and a theme system that respects user preference.
author: Satyam Kushwaha
content: A portfolio is a long conversation with future collaborators. It should load fast, show range, and quietly demonstrate taste.

For the rebuild I leaned on plain CSS custom properties for theming, Tailwind for layout, and a tiny IntersectionObserver hook for the reveal animations. No framer-motion, no headless UI — just the platform, honestly used.

The animations are intentionally restrained: a 40px translate, a soft fade, staggered by about 120ms across siblings. Anything flashier starts to feel like the site is selling the animation instead of the work.
If you're building your own, resist the urge to add a library until you've tried the CSS and a hook first. Most of what we import is vestigial.

title: From CRA to Vite — Notes From the Middle of a Migration
excerpt: Why I considered migrating, what changed, and the three small gotchas that cost me an afternoon each.
author: Satyam Kushwaha
content: CRA served us well, but dev-server cold starts and config escape hatches were adding up. Vite's ESM-native pipeline is a big step up for everyday DX.
The migration itself was mostly renames: index.html moves to the root, entry points get a module script, and environment variables pivot from REACT_APP_ to VITE_.
Three things bit me: SVG imports (need vite-plugin-svgr), process.env references in libraries, and a jest config that needed to move to Vitest.
After two days I had faster HMR, a 40% smaller bundle, and no more transitive audit noise. Worth it.

title: Three Hooks I Reach For Every Single Day
excerpt: useReveal, useScrollSpy, useTheme — the small custom hooks that earn their weight in every React app I build.
author: Satyam Kushwaha
content: Custom hooks are the cheapest abstraction in React. The trick is to keep them sharp — one responsibility, clear return shape.
useReveal wraps IntersectionObserver and returns [ref, visible]. I use it for every scroll-in animation on this site.
useScrollSpy takes a list of section IDs and returns the active one — perfect for highlighting the current nav link.
useTheme persists a theme to localStorage and sets a data-theme attribute on <body>. It's five lines. It changes the whole feel.
